data.title: Vance wades into Trump's feud with Pope Leo
data.description:
Vice President Vance downplayed concerns about President Trump’s ongoing feud with Pope Leo XIV late Monday, suggesting it was not newsworthy.

Trump recently clashed with the leader of the Catholic Church over the war in Iran, calling the pope “weak on crime” in a social media post on Sunday. 

Vance, who is promoting his upcoming book about his conversion to Catholicism, dismissed the backlash over the exchange in an interview with Fox News.

“I don’t think that it’s particularly newsworthy, but I certainly think that in some cases it would be best for the Vatican to stick to matters of morality, to stick to matters of what’s going on in the Catholic Church and let the president of the United States stick to dictating American public policy,” he said.

The vice president also addressed Trump’s controversial AI-generated post online that portrayed the president as what appeared to be Jesus. The president later deleted the picture after facing backlash from some of his political supporters and told CBS News that he thought the image portrayed him as a doctor. 

Vance said the post was a “joke” and that Trump took it down “because he realized that a lot of people weren’t understanding his humor.”

“I think the president of the United States likes to mix it up on social media, and I actually think that’s one of the good things about the president is that he’s not filtered,” the vice president continued. “He doesn’t send everything through a communications professional. He actually reaches out directly to the people.”

Pope Leo has urged the U.S. and Iran to “return to the negotiating table” and celebrated Trump’s announcement of a temporary ceasefire deal earlier this month. Vance and a team of U.S. diplomats failed to reach a long-term agreement during marathon talks with an Iranian delegation in Pakistan over the weekend. 

The conflict has driven up energy costs across the globe, sending the average price of gasoline, crude oil and fertilizer sky high in the U.S. 

Vance told Fox News on Monday evening that the administration “know[s] the American people are hurting” and vowed to continue negotiations. 

“I do think that we’re in a place where we’ve accomplished our objectives, we can start to wind this thing down,” he said. “I’d much rather wind this thing down with a big successful negotiation, but regardless, the president said this is not going to go on forever.”

The vice president added, “That’s never his intention, and I think because of that, the energy prices, while painful, are not going to be around forever.”

data.title: Pancreatic cancer pill shown to nearly double overall survival time, drug company says
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A pancreatic cancer drug is showing promising signs of a possible breakthrough after a clinical trial found it nearly doubled the length of survival among patients.

In a press release Monday, Revolution Medicines shared the findings of its Phase 3 clinical trial, which evaluated the drug Daraxonrasib in patients with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma who had been previously treated.

The company reports the drug, taken orally once a day, showed “clinically meaningful improvements” in progression-free survival and overall survival compared with standard-of-care cytotoxic chemotherapy delivered intravenously. Specifically, the drug showed a median overall survival of 13.2 months compared to 6.7 months for chemotherapy.

“For patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer, new treatment options are urgently needed to increase survival time and improve quality of life,” Dr. Brian M. Wolpin, principal investigator for the RASolute 302 trial, said.

He added, “I believe that this new approach is a very important advance for the field that I expect will be practice-changing for physicians and improve the care for patients with previously treated metastatic pancreatic cancer.”

The drug is designed to stop RAS mutations, which drive tumor growth and are found in 90% of pancreatic cancer cases.

According to the Pancreatic Cancer Action Network, the five-year survival rate for pancreatic cancer remains flat at 13 percent and just 8 percent for people diagnosed with the most common form of pancreatic cancer, pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

Revolution Medicines says it intends to submit the findings of this trial to global regulatory authorities, including the Food and Drug Administration, as part of a future New Drug Application under a Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher.

data.title: The Movement: Anatomy of an anti-amnesty pile-on
data.description:
Right-wing social media and commentary last week was dominated by infighting and outrage over the Dignity Act, a bipartisan immigration reform bill — and it seemed to come out of nowhere.

 Rep. Maria Elvira Salazar (R-Fla.), the designer of the compromise measure that pairs border security boosts with a new legal status for non-criminal migrants who entered before 2021, publicly sparred with critics of her legislation. Those hardline conservatives — numerous Republican members of Congress, influential commentators and conservative social media accounts — fumed for most of the last week about Republicans embracing what they deemed to be “amnesty” (which Salazar disputed). 

But the bill is not new. It was first introduced years ago, and re-introduced last year. It is nowhere near having a markup, let alone a vote, and has essentially no chance of advancing in the current Congress. It has no buy-in from leadership. 

I, and others on Capitol Hill and beyond with whom I chatted about the uproar, were left wondering: Why is this even a thing?

The episode is indicative of a frequent freak-out dynamic on the right, in which the most hardline purists take charge to beat down those in their own party seen as “squishy” on an issue.

Influencers and Republican members have posted criticisms of the Dignity Act from time to time, without it ever turning into a multi-day focus. But this time, there seems to have been just the perfect storm of an opening in the news cycle combined with pent-up frustration from Republicans over immigration enforcement. 

What seemed to have sparked the uproar this time was cosponsor Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) advocating for the bill in a weekend Fox News appearance. Host Brian Kilmeade commented that he thought Trump could be open to the bill, saying the president had told him he would be interested in “long-term work visas” for migrants.

A few conservative accounts picked up on the interview. Conservative influencer Mila Joy had posted the exact same language slamming “RINOs in Congress” who support the Dignity Act multiple times over the last year, but it really got traction last week after the Lawler interview. A wave of influencers followed.

The uproar came at a time when Congress was out of session, and accelerated after a ceasefire was announced with Iran on Tuesday night, and there was a lull in the news cycle.

And taking aim at the Dignity Act is a way for conservatives who have pent-up frustration over the Trump administration delivering on mass deportations and how an off-ramp for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown is being handled to blow off steam without criticizing President Trump or Republican leadership directly.

Public sparring on social media between Salazar and other members like Rep. Brandon Gill (R-Texas) kept the controversy going, as did more TV appearances.

Lawler returned to Fox on Laura Ingraham’s show for what the host said from the top would “not be a fun interview,” with the segment largely devolving into crosstalk as Ingraham repeatedly asserted Lawler was misrepresenting his own bill.

Inghram had Gill on as a guest the following night, where he argued the Dignity Act was an “unforgivable political betrayal” of those who voted for Trump.

The big question: Is the uproar leading to political consequences for the bill’s cosponsors, or dimming prospects for the bill?

Well, the office of one cosponsor told me that while there was an uptick in constituent interactions over the Dignity Act due to the social media backlash, it was much lower than inquiries about the Iran war and DHS funding, and lower than a typical uptick that coincides with top news items.

There might be some more action on the issue with lawmakers back in town this week. Salazar told me in an interview that she plans to reach out to Gill directly to talk to him about the legislation, and she is having a press conference with the National Association of Manufacturers in support of the bill later this week.

And underlying tension between moderate Republicans aiming to combat a decline in support for the GOP among the Hispanic and Latino and hard-line immigration restrictionists is not going away any time soon. 

Further reading on the details: Sudden GOP infighting explodes over bipartisan immigration reform bill, by me and my colleague Rebecca Beitsch… Why MAGA has a problem with Maria, by Ali Biano in Politico…

Welcome to The Movement, a weekly newsletter looking at the influences and debates on the right in Washington. I’m Emily Brooks, House leadership reporter at The Hill. 

Send me tips, comments, and suggestions: ebrooks@thehill.com.

Follow me on X: @emilybrooksnews

Not already on the list? Subscribe here

PRO-LIFERS JOLTED BY REPORT ON SPEAKER JOHNSON

A story in the Washington Examiner last week sent a jolt through the anti-abortion movement: “Mike Johnson to skip defunding Planned Parenthood in next party-line bill.” 

The Speaker, it said, plans to leave out a provision of the next party-line budget reconciliation bill to renew a ban on Medicaid dollars going to groups like Planned Parenthood before it expires on July 4.

The report shocked many in the “pro-life” advocacy sphere, given Johnson’s long record of aggressively pushing for anti-abortion policies.

Johnson’s office was quite frustrated by the story, thinking the angle misrepresented the overall dynamic in Congress, including by singling out the Speaker over a bill that will originate in the Senate.

The next reconciliation bill will be used to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol to end the record-long Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown — a last-resort measure after failing to find agreement with Democrats who refuse to vote to fund DHS without reforms. Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and other GOP leaders want to keep the bill very limited so Republicans in Congress can swiftly move it through the process to get to Trump’s desk by June 1. Priorities that were set for a potential second reconciliation bill are being pushed to a potential third bill in the fall, leaders have said. Plus, the bill is being crafted in the Senate, not in the House. Some of those details were in the story.

At least one anti-abortion group reached out to Johnson’s office about the report and felt satisfied by Johnson’s support for extending the provision.

John Mize, CEO of Americans United for Life, told me in a statement: “Speaker Johnson championed defunding abortion providers last year, and his team relayed to us that his support has not changed for a second reconciliation. Attempts to sow discord and prevent extended defunding from being included must be put aside. The House GOP must fall in line behind the Speaker’s steady, pro-life leadership and extend federal defunding before it expires this summer.”

But with a third reconciliation bill not being set until the fall, past the July 4 deadline — if it happens at all — the episode could be an early indication of lobbying pressure that will be put on Republicans to stick other priorities into the ICE-Border Patrol reconciliation bill.

TRUMP BREAKS UP WITH THE PODCASTERS

President Trump solidified his break-up with allies-turned-critics in the podcast world last week, going after Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly, Candace Owens, and Alex Jones, who have all criticized him on Iran — saying they had “Low IQs,” were “stupid people,” and “Third Rate Podcasters.”

Carlson, Trump said, “was a broken man when he got fired from Fox.” Trump said he hopes French First Lady Brigitte Macron wins money over Candace’s claims she’s a man. And he said Jones deserved to lose his fortune over his false Sandy Hook conspiracy theories.

How are the podcasters responding? 

Carlson started selling hats that say “Low IQ” and published a newsletter insinuating that Trump might be being blackmailed by Israel.

Owens started selling merch with quotes from Trump’s post — including a mug and a sweatshirt that said “Actually, to me, the First ‘Lady’ of France is a far more beautiful woman than Candace” — and made her next show an “open letter” to Trump relaying criticisms of Trump’s foreign policy from his former supporters, and asserting he belongs to the “Epstein class.”

Jones said he was now going “scorched earth” on Trump. “You should be kissing my a–. Instead you s— all over me and rub it in …. I’m not looking for a fight, but if you want one you came to the right place.”

Related: MAGA media’s great unraveling, by Zachary Basu in Axios.

ON MY CALENDAR

  • Tuesday, April 14: Americans for Tax Reform hosts its annual “Tax Day Eve” press conference on Capitol Hill, featuring GOP Reps. Lisa McClain (Mich.), Beth Van Duyne (Texas), Mike Kelly (Pa.), and the organization’s president Grover Norquist.
  • Wednesday, April 15: The American Legislative Exchange Council hosts an event in the Capitol for its “Rich States, Poor States” rankings, with Utah Senate President J. Stuart Adams and Utah Speaker Mike Schultz.
  • Saturday, April 18 and Tuesday, April 21: Citizen’s Climate Lobby hosts a conservative conference and lobby day aimed at targeting Republicans on climate policy. Details here.
  • Wednesday, April 29: Susan B. Anthony Pro-Life America hosts a gala that will feature Sen. Ted Cruz.

THREE MORE THINGS

  1. Rep. Marlin Stutzman (R-Ind.), a Freedom Caucus member, publicly slammed former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) for calling for the 25th Amendment to be used against Trump over his threat against Iran. Stutzman said Greene is starting to sound like Rep. Rashida Tlaib (D-Mich.) or Tucker Carlson. Stutzman told me of his decision to speak up: “These folks are off the rails … It’s definitely affecting young people. I hear that and see that … They’re focused strictly on Israel more than they are focused on other countries that are killing Christians and or invading other countries as well.”
  2. President Trump’s Truth Social post with an image seeming to depict him as Jesus sent a jolt through the Christian conservative sphere, with commentators like Riley Gaines and Michael Knwoles balking at the post. Trump later took down the post, and told reporters he thought it depicted him as a doctor “and had to do with the Red Cross.” Trump also later told CBS: “I didn’t listen to Riley Gaines. I’m not a big fan of Riley, actually.”
  3. Paul Dans, who was a key leader of the Heritage Foundation’s Project 2025 and was briefly in the Office of Personnel Management in Trump’s first term, ended his long-shot bid to primary Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) on Friday. Dans was in Washington last week, and had attended a party for The American Conservative magazine.

WHAT I’M READING

  • Wall Street Journal’s Jordan Kranse and Eliza Collins: We Had Mocktails With the Women Behind the MAHA Movement
  • Washington Post’s Alec Dent: Heritage president toasted editor of controversial right-wing magazine
  • The Daily Signal’s Elizabeth Troutman Mitchell: White House Strong-Arms 2 More States on AI
  • New York Times’s Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman: How Trump Took the U.S. to War With Iran
  • NBC News’s Jane C. Timm: How the White House tried — and failed — to push a candidate out of a Republican state Senate primary in Indiana

data.title: When scientists believed Artemis II space travel was impossible
data.description:
A hundred years ago, the scientific consensus asserted that space travel wasn’t just difficult — it was physically impossible. The successful return of the Artemis II astronauts has proven, once again, that the science was wrong.

The first nonfiction book in English to explore and promote the possibility of interplanetary space flight was published in 1931. “The Conquest of Space” by David Lasser, president of the recently formed American Interplanetary Society, was privately published because so many scientists and publishers at the time believed that space travel wasn’t just science fiction but science fantasy.

The American Interplanetary Society had been formed by Lasser and a few others in April 1930 for the purpose of discussing the possibility of space flight and conducting experiments on the best types of fuels to achieve the goal. In 1934, the society changed its name to the American Rocket Society in hopes of appealing to more people, who might be intrigued by rockets but thought interplanetary travel was an unrealistic dream.

The skepticism came because many scientists believed that a reaction to a rocket engine would only happen if there was resistance, such as the ground or air, to the thrust coming from the motor. In other words, they thought Newton’s Third Law of Motion — that for every action (force) in nature, there is an equal and opposite reaction — simply would not apply in the vacuum of space.

Defenders claimed that Newton’s law still applied regardless of whether there is resistance. That is why all three of the pre-war English books on space flight — Lasser’s “The Conquest of Space,” Chas G. Philp’s “Stratosphere and Rocker Power” (1935) and the founder of the British Interplanetary Society P.E. Cleator’s “Rockets Through Space” (1936) — included discussions on why Newton’s Third Law would still apply.

Everyone knew that traditional airplanes, which used propellers to force air over the wings, would not work in space because there was no air. Space-flight proponents believed that a mixture of combustible fuels would produce an action causing a reaction — i.e., the rocket moving forward. In fact, these rocket “engines” were often referred to as “reaction motors.” 

Not all scientists were skeptics. Lasser’s book includes an introduction by Professor Harold H. Shelton, then a professor of physics at New York University and science editor of The New York Herald Tribune. He wrote in the introduction, “Most people do not take the rocket seriously,” adding that many think “the rocket as a mode of propulsion is ridiculous and that all rocket enthusiasts are mental defectives.”  

Although Lasser’s book was the first in English, there were scientists in other countries who preceded him.  

The Russian Konstantin Tsiolkovsky (1857-1935) was one of the early pioneers in astronautics, though he mostly worked and wrote in obscurity. And there was Professor Hermann Oberth (1894-1989), who taught Dr. Wernher von Braun and worked with him on the German V-2 rocket, later following von Braun to the U.S. Oberth wrote perhaps the first detailed description of rockets and space flight, in German, in “The Rocket into Planetary Space” in 1923. He also played a consulting role in Fritz Lang’s 1929 silent film classic about a flight to the moon, “Woman in the Moon.”

We should also recognize the American Professor Robert H. Goddard (1882-1945) and his groundbreaking work, especially on liquid fuels. Lasser encouraged Goddard to be part of his work with the American Rocket Society, but Goddard was very private and preferred to work alone.

While U.S. publishers were reluctant to release Lasser’s book, at least one British publisher was less so: Hurst and Blackett of London. Science fiction and space science writer Arthur C. Clarke wrote in an introduction to the 2002 reprint of “The Conquest of Space” that in 1931, “the 14-year-old Arthur Clarke is walking with his beloved Aunt Nellie past a bookstore in Minehead.” He saw “The Conquest of Space” in the window and it caught his attention. Even though he had been a big reader of science fiction, “it had never occurred to him that space travel might one day be for real. So he persuaded Aunt Nellie to purchase the book, and it literally changed his life.” 

It also changed the lives of all those who live on earth and, perhaps soon, those who live on the moon. Artemis II reminds us of just how much science and our perspective can change. 

Merrill Matthews is the Texas state chair of Our Republican Legacy.

data.title: Cough drops sold nationwide recalled: FDA
data.description:
NEW YORK (WPIX) — A China-based company is voluntarily recalling numerous cough drop products sold across the U.S., according to the Food and Drug Administration.

The FDA recommended the recall after a manufacturing facility inspection on Aug. 15, 2025, resulted in observations “that may bear on product quality,” the agency noted. However, specific details were not disclosed.

Xiamen Kang Zhongyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd., headquartered in Xiamen, China, initiated the recall on March 20, 2026. On Friday, the FDA classified the recall as Class II.

The products included in the recall are:

  • Exchange Select, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Honey Lemon Flavor Cough Drops, 30-count bag
    • Lot number: 20241030, Exp Date: 10/30/2026, UPC: 614299398870
  • Exchange Select, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Menthol Flavor, 30-count bag
    • Lot number: 20241030, Exp Date: 10/30/2026, UPC: 614299398887
  • Caring Mill, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Cherry, 90-count bag, distributed by: FSA Store Inc.
    • Lot number: 20241030, Exp Date: 10/30/2026, UPC: 810025928407
  • Discount Drug Mart Food Market, Cough Drops, Menthol Cough Suppressant Anesthetic, Honey Lemon, 30-count bag, distributed by Drug Mart-Food Fair
    • Lot number: 20241030, Exp Date: 10/30/2026, UPC: 093351037092
  • Discount Drug Mart Food Market, Cough Drops, Menthol Cough Suppressant Anesthetic, 30-count bag, distributed by Drug Mart-Food Fair
    • Lot number: 20241030, Exp Date: 10/30/2026, UPC: 093351037085
  • MGC Health, Menthol Cough Suppressant, Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Sugar Free, Honey Lemon, 25-count bag, distributed by Medical Group Care, LLC.
    • Lot number: 20240524, Exp Date: 05/24/2026; Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026; Lot number: 20240730, Exp Date: 07/30/2026, UPC: 383173000085
  • MGC Health, Menthol Cough Suppressant, Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Honey Lemon, 30-count bag, distributed by Medical Group Care, LLC.
    • Lot number: 20240524, Exp Date: 05/24/2026, UPC: 383173000047
  • QC Quality Choice, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Sugar Free, Black Cherry Flavor, 25-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026, UPC: 635515993372
  • QC Quality Choice, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Cherry Flavor, 30-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240524, Exp Date: 05/24/2026; Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026, UPC: 635515993372.
  • QC Quality Choice, Pectin Oral Demulcent, Throat Soothing Drops, Creamy Strawberry Flavor, 30-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026, UPC: 635515999398
  • QC Quality Choice, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Honey Lemon Flavor, 30-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026, UPC: 63551598673
  • QC Quality Choice, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Menthol Flavor, 30-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026; Lot number: 20240524, Exp Date: 05/24/2026, UPC: 635515986718
  • QC Quality Choice, Menthol Cough Suppressant Oral Anesthetic, Cough Drops, Vanilla Honey Flavor, 30-count bag, distributed by CDMA, Inc.
    • Lot number: 20240720, Exp Date: 07/20/2026, UPC: 635515999411

The FDA has designated this as a Class II recall, meaning exposure to the product may cause temporary or medically reversible adverse health consequences.

data.title: Roblox to roll out new age-based accounts, parental controls
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Roblox, a popular gaming app, announced Monday it is introducing new age-based accounts and expanded parental controls for users under 16.

The new age-based accounts are called “Roblox Kids” for users 5 to 8 years old and “Roblox Select” for users 9 to 15 years old.

“When they roll out in early June, these accounts will more closely align content access, communication settings, and parental controls with a user’s age,” the company said in a press release. “We’re also establishing an ongoing selection process for games available to users under 16.”

How will the accounts work?

For Roblox Kids accounts, the company said access will be limited to games with a “Minimal or Mild content maturity label” that have passed its three-step selection process. The accounts will also have communication features disabled by default and the design layout will have a distinct background color to indicate the type of account it is.

Meanwhile, the ages of users for the Roblox Select accounts will be determined through the company’s universal age-check system or by a verified parent and will be limited to games with content maturity levels “up to and including Moderate.” Default communications settings will remain unchanged for ages 9-15.

How will expanded parental controls work?

With the update to parental controls, Roblox said parents will be able to block specific games and manage direct chat settings through age 15.

In addition, parents will be able to approve access to specific games that are not under the default setting of their child’s account type.

Push to address safety concerns

TechCrunch reports that the move comes after Roblox implemented mandatory age checks in January for all users who want to access chats on the app.

The gaming app has come under scrutiny in recent months after public data revealed the platform flagged more than 13,000 attempts to exploit minors in 2023. Predators are accused of using Roblox in-game currency to coerce and manipulate kids into unwanted sexual acts. In response, Los Angeles County filed a civil lawsuit against the platform.

Roblox said that in addition to parental controls, it would add new layers of protection, including AI-powered technology that can verify a user’s age, which could help keep kids from accessing explicit material.

data.title: As oil prices surge, bringing back telework is common sense
data.description:
The U.S. is now facing a major disruption in the global oil market caused by the Iran war. It is driving up gas prices, putting pressure on working families and the broader economy. Oil prices affect everything from farming to shipping to the cost of driving to Disney World, leaving Americans with less money for everything else.

At a moment like this, policymakers should be looking for immediate, practical ways to reduce strain and lower prices where possible. One such step is already within reach: President Trump can expand telework across the federal workforce as an immediate, demand-side measure to combat soaring gas prices by reducing national fuel consumption.

Reports from the International Energy Agency have identified “working from home remotely” as a policy that governments can implement immediately. By reducing daily commutes, telework directly lowers fuel demand at a time when global supply is constrained.

With more than 2 million federal employees and roughly 50 percent eligible to telework, the federal government is uniquely positioned to lead by example. Road transportation accounts for roughly 45 percent of global oil demand and reducing commuting-related fuel consumption can help ease pressure on an already strained system. The IEA estimates that working from home for just three extra days a week can reduce fuel consumption from passenger vehicles by between 2 percent and 6 percent.

Telework for federal employees is not a new concept. Prior to the administration eliminating telework for federal employees and violating labor contracts last year, telework was widely used across the federal government to retain expertise, improve productivity and save taxpayer dollars. Approximately half of the federal civilian workforce, roughly 1.2 million employees, were either fully remote or teleworking part-time. On any given workday, a significant share of the workforce was already reducing fuel use simply by not commuting.

Just as importantly, telework enables federal employees to continue delivering on their core missions efficiently and without disruption. At the Environmental Protection Agency, that means ensuring work continues to protect clean air, safe drinking water and public health in communities across the country. 

The federal workforce already has the technology, infrastructure and experience to expand telework quickly, built in large part during the COVID-19 pandemic. Employees with on-site responsibilities, such as Transportation Security Administration officers or National Park Service staff, will, of course, continue reporting in person to serve the public. But for those who are eligible, expanded telework is a practical and immediate option.

At a time when global energy markets are under strain, reducing consumption at home is one way to improve resilience and limit the impact on American families. Several Asian nations, including Vietnam, Thailand and Pakistan, have already implemented work-from-home policies for government employees to reduce fuel consumption and conserve supplies because of the Iran War. In addition to reducing commuting, telework can also lower energy use associated with large office buildings, further contributing to overall demand reduction.

The question is not whether telework is feasible. The federal government has already demonstrated that it is. The question is whether the president and his administration are willing to use the tools already available to them.

Expanding telework is not a long-term solution to global energy challenges. But it is a simple, immediate step that can reduce unnecessary fuel consumption, ease pressure on working families and ensure that the federal government continues to operate effectively during a period of uncertainty.

At this moment, that kind of practical action is exactly what is needed. So, what is holding the Trump administration back?

 Justin Chen is president of AFGE Council 238.

data.title: Delta doubles down on premium seats as airlines target big spenders
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Delta Air Lines is doubling down on high-paying travelers with new business-class suites, as major carriers focus more on big-spending passengers than simply filling seats.

The airline unveiled its next-generation Delta One suite on Monday, part of a broader push to upgrade premium cabins with more privacy, larger screens and extra onboard perks.

The suites, featuring longer sleeping space, extra cushioning and a “cinema quality” 4K screen, will debut on the airline’s new Airbus A350-1000 aircraft, which is scheduled to arrive in early 2027.

Delta’s emphasis on higher-paying passengers is also evident in the cabin mix, with half of the seats on the A350-1000 dedicated to premium customers.

The shift has become a common example of what some describe as a “K-shaped” economy, with higher-income consumers continuing to spend while others pull back. Delta executives have embraced that framing.

“Our consumer is really healthy,” Delta CEO Ed Bastian said in a recent interview. “We live at the top end of that K that people talk about, the premium end of the K.”

The airline’s latest earnings underscore that strategy, with premium revenue up 14 percent year over year.

Rivals, including American Airlines and United Airlines, have followed suit, shifting their focus toward higher-margin premium seats.

Last month, United announced a series of upgrades centered on premium seating, which it said has increased 40 percent per North American departure since 2021. The airline has also introduced tiered fare categories within its premium cabins.

The recent investments suggest airlines are betting that higher-income consumers will continue to fly, even as rising jet fuel costs tied to the war in Iran push fares and fees higher.

Airfares rise amid Iran conflict, but premium demand is strong

On last week’s earnings call, Bastian said premium consumers have become “more immune to the headlines” and are not “delaying their investment in the experience economy.”

“As difficult as it is to see what’s going on with the conflict in the Middle East, I’m not sure that our premium customers are feeling affected by that,” the Delta CEO said.

It is less clear whether higher costs will weigh on everyday consumers’ appetite for travel.

Data from Kayak shows domestic and international airfares have risen since the war began and are higher than a year ago.

Major U.S. carriers have also raised baggage fees for economy passengers, while travelers with premium seats have largely been spared.

A third of respondents in a recent survey said they are traveling less this year because of rising costs, while another 25 percent are opting for cheaper or closer destinations, according to polling from The Points Guy and YouGov.

Meanwhile, 31 percent said higher prices were not influencing their travel decisions — a group airlines are likely counting on to keep spending.

For those buying premium seats, the difference can run into the thousands of dollars.

A round-trip, direct flight from Atlanta to Amsterdam offers one example. On Delta, a basic main fare costs roughly $1,100 for a midweek flight a month from now. Premium Select on the same flight is nearly $3,400, while a Delta One seat tops $8,500.

Delta’s latest push into premium extends beyond new aircraft. The airline said Monday it is also upgrading its older A330-200 and A330-300 fleet with suites that include privacy doors, and expects 90% of its Delta One seats to feature doors by 2030.

Main cabins will also see upgrades on Delta, including an additional inch of legroom, larger setback screens and Bluetooth connectivity as the company invests in its fleet.

data.title: Trump angers Catholics in targeting Pope Leo XIV over Iran war pushback
data.description:
President Trump is escalating a rare feud with Pope Leo XIV over pushback on the Iran war, a move that could isolate the president’s Catholic voting bloc and complicate the Catholic members of his administration, including the first lady.

Trump posted a lengthy Truth Social post Sunday night calling the pope “weak” on crime and insisting the Catholic leader believed it was OK for Iran to have a nuclear weapon, though Leo has not expressed such a notion.

The pope in return, said he had “no fear” of the Trump administration, expressing rare defiance of the White House.

The openly antagonizing message to the leader of a church with more than 1 billion members proves another unprecedented moment by Trump in his second term.

Ashley McGuire, senior fellow with the Catholic Association, said seeing Trump’s comments about the pope was a “combination of disappointment and confusion.”

“It doesn’t make sense politically for Trump to be attacking the pope, especially when the Catholic vote was the difference between 2020 and 2024, so I don’t understand why he would, in my opinion, gratuitously attack the leader of 1.4 billion Catholics,” McGuire said.

“President Trump was treating him like a political rival, and he’s just not,” she said.

The president’s feud with the first American pope does carry a political risk for him ahead of the 2026 midterms where Republicans are vying to keep control of the House and the Senate.

“I think he will regret these remarks politically in the midterms,” said Christopher Hale, a democratic operative and publisher of the Substack “Letters from Leo.” “And I think who will regret the remarks most is [Vice President] J.D. Vance and his potential campaign for president.”

Vance is one of several Catholic members of Trump’s administration, in addition to Secretary of State Marco Rubio. First lady Melania Trump is also Catholic.

Leo shocked the world by being elected as the first American pontiff in May. Since then, he’s been relatively mum about mentioning his country of origin or the president by name, including in his statements condemning the Iran war.

“He’s been clear from the outset that he wants to be the pope of 1.4 billion Catholics and not have a particular American bias, either in intervention or he doesn’t want to become a pundit about American politics,” Hale said. “He gets involved when it gets close to core gospel values that affect a large loss of humanity. So that for him is two issues during his first year, it’s the Iran war and migration, and that’s where we’re seeing him speak out.”

On social media, the pope has been calling for peace amid the war with Iran, saying in one post earlier this month that “God does not bless any conflict.”

“Anyone who is a disciple of Christ, the Prince of Peace, is never on the side of those who once wielded the sword and today drop bombs,” he wrote. “Military action will not create space for freedom or times of #Peace, which comes only from the patient promotion of coexistence and dialogue among peoples.”

On Saturday, he denounced the “delusion of omnipotence” that led to the continuation of the war.

“Enough of the idolatry of self and money!” he said. “Enough of the display of power! Enough of war!”

Trump told reporters aboard Air Force One on Sunday he’s “not a fan” of the pope.

But the president went much further in a Truth Social post to say he likes the pope’s brother Louis Prevost “much better” because he supports Trump’s MAGA movement.

“I don’t want a Pope who thinks it’s OK for Iran to have a Nuclear Weapon. I don’t want a Pope who thinks it’s terrible that America attacked Venezuela,” he wrote, adding, “I don’t want a Pope who criticizes the President of the United States because I’m doing exactly what I was elected.”

“Leo should be thankful because, as everyone knows, he was a shocking surprise,” he wrote. “He wasn’t on any list to be Pope, and was only put there by the Church because he was an American, and they thought that would be the best way to deal with President Donald J. Trump. If I wasn’t in the White House, Leo wouldn’t be in the Vatican.”

On Monday, Trump said he wouldn’t apologize to the pope after facing conservative backlash for the post.

Trump also defended a photo he posted on Truth Social that was made with AI and depicted him as Jesus healing a sick person. He took it down after it was heavily criticized.

Leo for his part on Monday said he’s “not afraid of the Trump administration or of speaking out loudly about the message of the Gospel, which is what the Church works for.”

“I will continue to speak out strongly against war, seeking to promote peace, promoting dialogue and multilateralism among states to find solutions to problems,” he said while embarking on an 11-day trip to Africa, according to The Associated Press.

Trump’s comments were criticized broadly.

Bishop Robert Barron, a member of Trump’s Religious Liberty Commission, slammed his comments as “entirely inappropriate and disrespectful.”

“No President in my lifetime has shown a greater dedication to defending our first liberty,” he wrote. “All that said, I think the President owes the Pope an apology.”

Archbishop Paul S. Coakley, president of the U.S. Conference of Catholic Bishops, said in a statement on social media that he was “disheartened that the President chose to write such disparaging words about the Holy Father.”

“Pope Leo is not his rival; nor is the Pope a politician,” he wrote. “He is the Vicar of Christ who speaks from the truth of the Gospel and for the care of souls.”

Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni also weighed in, saying she finds Trump’s comments “unacceptable.”

“The Pope is the head of the Catholic Church, and it is right and normal that he calls for peace and condemns every form of war,” she wrote.

data.title: Post-communist Cuba will need an export-led recovery
data.description:
The slow-motion implosion of the Cuban economy is accelerating. While Venezuela has provided Cuba with heavily subsidized (practically free) oil, the removal of Nicolas Maduro as president combined with Russia’s reluctance to contribute substantial financial support to Cuba, means it is just a matter of time before total collapse occurs.

Dismantling a Marxist regime is no easy task, especially after 67 years of control. But anyone well-versed in Cuba’s culture and history knows well that Cubans are more pragmatic than ideological. “Let’s make a deal” will be the mantra going forward.

Arguably the greatest challenges to a post-communist government in Cuba will not be political but economic. Up to 89 percent of Cubans live in extreme poverty, with most households surviving on the margins due to severe shortages of food, medicine, and electricity. The country presently faces its worst economic crisis since the 1990s, forcing many residents into extreme hardship and prompting a massive exodus, with 78 percent planning to emigrate. 

Cuba’s exports have fallen by 75 percent since 2000, and GDP in 2026 will shrink by 7.2 percent. The average Cuban must rely on government rations that are often inadequate and limited while the average state salary is around $20 per month — roughly the U.S. price to have a Burger King Double Whopper meal with extra fries delivered.

Recognizably, post-communist Cuba will need massive financial resources to address the dire needs of the population. While the U.S., Canada, and the EU will stand ready to contribute, one should not expect a Marshall Plan for Cuba. As for foreign investment, Cubans in the diaspora will surely consider investment opportunities after the U.S. embargo and other restrictions are removed and Cuba’s investment, tax, and regulatory regime is radically revamped.

Nevertheless, “ethnic affinity” is insufficient reason for Cuban Americans to invest in Cuba. And if they are interested in offshore investment closer to home, then the Dominican Republic, Mexico, and Panama present better opportunities.

To propel the Cuban economy forward, an export-led recovery should be among the highest priorities. It is here that the four most attractive sectors are agriculture, tourism, health technology and information technology.

Cuba’s key agricultural and agribusiness strengths are a strong agro-ecological endowment for sugar, tobacco, coffee, fruit, and niche organics, as well as a large underemployed rural labor force. However, the nation suffers from chronic underinvestment and heavy dependence on imported inputs, along with obsolete machinery. With substantial new investment in rural roads and ports, joint ventures and contract farming, the potential for upside is huge.

A focus on tourism would capitalize on exceptional natural and cultural assets and an established reputation as a major tourism exporter in the region, including for medical, health, and wellness tourism. However, Cuba’s outdated hotel stock, underinvestment in airports, seaports, and local transport (not to mention service-quality gaps) are glaring impediments. The country also needs to promote diversification into higher-yield segments, such as boutique and heritage hotels. Cuba also needs to integrate with cruise lines, airlines, and online platforms to build multi-destination Caribbean itineraries.

A third promising sector for Cuba is health technology and pharma. The country possesses an established biotech and pharmaceutical base, with internationally recognized vaccines and products. It is one of only six countries that makes vaccines with a greater than 90 percent efficacy and holds 2,438 registered patents. BioCubaFarma, Cuba’s state-run biotech organization, houses 32 research institutes and manufacturing enterprises and exports to more than 50 countries.

With limited access to capital and critical imported inputs and cutting-edge equipment, Cuba’s health technology and pharma sector will remain severely handicapped. But once democracy comes to the island, sweeping change will usher in the investment, public-private partnerships, and the emergence of a health-tech cluster that will help propel the Cuban economy forward.

With respect to information technology and digital services, Cuba’s key strengths are an educated, relatively low-cost workforce with strong foundations in math, engineering, and computer science. Cuba can also tap its large diaspora in advanced IT markets (US, Spain, Latin America) to channel knowledge, contracts, and capital. However, with its historically poor broadband, restricted internet access, and underdeveloped digital payments and e‑government systems, it will require a sweeping overhaul and upgrade of the current installed technology infrastructure for Cuba to compete with well-developed regional rivals such as the Dominican Republic, Costa Rica and Panama.

As attractive as the four aforementioned sectors are, those contemplating doing business in a post-communist Cuba should do so with their eyes wide open. To quote Cuba’s national hero José Martí: “Day and night I always dream with open eyes.”

Cuba’s export success will depend upon its capacity to design, plan and implement a system of governance that fully embraces economic freedom, with tax, regulatory, judicial, and commercial policies that enshrine savings, productivity, investment and entrepreneurship. Nothing less will do.

Jerry Haar is a business professor at Florida International University and a fellow at both the Baratta Center for Global Business Education at Georgetown University and New York University’s Development Research Institute. He is also a member of the International Executive Resources Group.
